whatItMeasures: Hemoglobin is the iron-containing protein inside red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body and returns carbon dioxide back to the lungs; measuring it reflects the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity.

referenceRanges.source: WHO. Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia. 2011.
WHO. Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia. 2011.

causesOfHigh: Polycythemia vera (bone marrow disorder causing excess red blood cell production)
Living at high altitude (physiological adaptation to lower oxygen levels)
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other lung diseases
Heavy smoking (carbon monoxide displaces oxygen, stimulating more red cell production)
Severe dehydration (concentrated blood falsely raises hemoglobin)
Use of anabolic steroids or erythropoiesis-stimulating agents
causesOfLow: Iron deficiency anemia (most common cause worldwide)
Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency anemia
Bone marrow disorders (aplastic anemia, myelodysplastic syndrome)
Acute or chronic blood loss (surgery, heavy menstruation, GI bleeding)
Pregnancy (dilutional effect of increased plasma volume)

high: Enlarged spleen (fullness on the left side)
low: Shortness of breath, especially during activity
